title: Dichroic Raman probes for chiral edge modes
abstract: The identification and manipulation of charge-neutral fractionalized quasi-particles, in particular chiral edge modes (CEM), is a long-standing quest in physics. Remarkably, the microscopically mediated interaction between light and charge-neutral excitations in Mott-Hubbard insulators can take an identical form to the Raman coupling between light and particles with electric charge. However, since CEMs are Raman-inactive due to conservation of lattice momentum, Raman probes have been deemed unsuitable for their identification. Here, using the Kitaev quantum spin liquid (KSL) as an illustrative example, we demonstrate that the long-range correlated disorder inherent to a closed edge can lead to a Raman circular dichroism (RCD) signal that avoids suppression by linear and angular momentum selection rules, and exhibits a dependence on experimentally tunable length and energy scales that are characteristic of CEM. Having calculated the low-frequency RCD response of generic KSL, we argue that the interaction of the chiral matter fermion with the $\Ztwo$ boundary charge leaves a unique fingerprint of the KSL via the anisotropic Zeeman field dependence.
summary: This paper proposes a new way to use Raman circular dichroism to detect the elusive chiral edge modes in Kitaev quantum spin liquids. It shows that while these modes are normally Raman-inactive due to momentum conservation, the broken symmetry at real physical edges allows a detectable signal to emerge. The study highlights that the signal's dependence on the Zeeman field and edge geometry serves as a unique fingerprint for the spin liquid phase. This provides a potential experimental roadmap for identifying fractionalized excitations in topological materials.
main_problem: The difficulty of identifying and detecting charge-neutral chiral edge modes (CEM) in topological systems like Kitaev spin liquids using Raman spectroscopy, which is typically forbidden by momentum conservation selection rules.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that broken translational symmetry at realistic, curved edges allows for a Raman circular dichroism (RCD) signal that evades selection rules, providing a unique spectroscopic fingerprint of the Kitaev spin liquid phase.
method: Theoretical calculation of the low-frequency Raman circular dichroism response using the Loudon-Fleury operator and numerical analysis of a punctured disk geometry with disorder averaging.
model_or_system: The Kitaev quantum spin liquid (KSL) model on a honeycomb lattice, including effects of an out-of-plane magnetic field (Zeeman field) and boundary-induced symmetry breaking.
key_observables: Raman circular dichroism (RCD), Raman intensity (I++), two-particle density of states (2DOS), and Raman center of energy (RCE).
important_parameters: Bulk gap (Delta), Zeeman field (h), edge geometry/length scale (L), energy scale (EL), and anisotropy (kappa).
main_assumptions: Neglect of certain non-Kitaev terms on zig-zag edges due to sublattice imbalance; use of the Loudon-Fleury form for non-resonant Raman scattering.
figures_summary: Figure 1 sketches the experimental protocol with nano-structured holes; Figure 2 shows RCD in disorder-free and disordered disks; Figure 4 shows frequency dependence of RCD; Figure 5 illustrates RCE on hexagonal flakes; Figure 6 and 7 show Raman intensity dependence on Zeeman field and geometry.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of detecting CEMs, presents a theoretical framework using the Kitaev model, analyzes the impact of edge geometry and symmetry breaking on Raman selection rules, evaluates the robustness of the signal to disorder, and concludes with the implications for experimental identification of spin liquids.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Sketch of the proposed protocol whereby the Raman intensity is measured as the shape and number of holes is varied, thereby tuning the surface to bulk ratio. The Raman intensity at Raman shift frequencies below the bulk gap ∆ probes the chiral edge mode. As sketched on the right panel, in the KSL, the edge mode is a superposition of a chiral matter fermion with support at finite momenta between the Dirac points, and the Z2 boundary charge d.o.f.

FIG. 2. Raman circular dichrosim I+−−I+−of a punctured disk of a perfect Kitaev material. Panel (a) shows the case with no bond-matter hybridization for various κ up to the κ dependent ∆for a hexagonal disk with r1 = 6, r2 = 56a. The energetically allowed transitions are indicated by the vertical bars at the bottom, whose colors encode the angular momen- tum transfer as determined in the end matter. Panel (b) shows the case with bond matter hybridization h = 0.1J and κ = 0.12J for various defect densities described by equation (7) on a circular M = 12 disk with r1 = 36a and r2 = 46a.
